summary: Back in Japan, my family was involved in growing citrus fruits and exporting them to Manchuria. That connection made me consider going there at one point. However, my brother wanted me to come to America instead, so eventually I left for the United States rather than Manchuria.

My sister graduated from high school in 1915, the same year as the Panama–Pacific International Exposition in San Francisco. That year was significant as my father summoned her to America. The exposition was a notable event in San Francisco at that time.

I don't have direct memories of the Sino-Japanese War. I only learned about it later from my teachers when I started school. It was part of the history I grew up hearing about but did not experience firsthand.

Like the Sino-Japanese War, I didn't remember the Russo-Japanese War directly. My knowledge came from what teachers told us in school. My great grandfather, who was a samurai, did go to war in 1879, which was before these conflicts.

My family religion was Buddhism, and I was raised with that faith. Even after coming to America, I remained Buddhist until my mother encouraged me to become Christian. Buddhism was the foundation of my early spiritual life.

My sister became a believer in Tenrikyo when she was seriously ill and searching for spiritual comfort. At that time, she was unsure which religion to trust, but she found solace in Tenrikyo. It was a significant part of her faith journey while our mother was raising us alone.

I left from Kobe on the ship Nihon Maru in early March 1921. We stayed in Kobe for a day before continuing the voyage to America. Kobe was the last port I saw in Japan before coming to the United States.

I traveled to America aboard the Nihon Maru in 1921. It was a small ship and that voyage was its last from Japan. The trip took almost a month, and it was rough at times with storms and seasickness among passengers and crew.

We arrived in San Francisco at night on March 30, 1921, and landed the next day. I stayed there for about three to four days before taking a train south to Fresno. San Francisco was my first experience in America, and I had dreamed of living in a stately brick house upon arrival.

After arriving in San Francisco, I went to Fresno where my mother and brother were living. Fresno was where I started working in agriculture, pruning grapes and learning farm labor. It was also where I was evacuated during World War II and stayed in a makeshift assembly center.

I came from Wakayama Prefecture, and many people from there came to America in those days. It was not considered reckless to come from Wakayama to the US. I also organized the Wakayama Kenjin-Kai in Central California before World War II.

In Central California, many Japanese immigrants were from Hiroshima Prefecture. They had organized the Hiroshima Kenjin-Kai, which was quite active before World War II. I knew of their efforts as I organized the Wakayama Kenjin-Kai in the same area.

When World War II broke out, we were evacuated from our home and placed in an assembly center near Fresno. We were only allowed to bring limited belongings and were transported by bus from Selma. The churches were very kind to us during this difficult time, and I was grateful to belong to a Christian church then.

The Japanese Association was the main political organization helping Japanese immigrants in those days. They mediated troubles between Japanese and Americans and had local chapters as well as a federation. It was an important support network for us in Central California.

I organized the Wakayama Kenjin-Kai in Central California to support people from my home prefecture. There were also Kenjin-Kai for other prefectures like Hiroshima. Unfortunately, the Wakayama Kenjin-Kai became extinct during World War II evacuation and was not reorganized after the war due to the busy rebuilding period and local opposition.

My wife had been attending a Methodist church since she was young. Although I was originally Buddhist, I was baptized and became a Christian before our marriage in 1932, partly because my mother encouraged us to embrace Christianity in America. We were married in a Christian church, reflecting the influence of Methodism in our lives.

During World War II evacuation, we were taken by bus from Selma to a makeshift assembly center near Fresno. The local white churches in Selma provided refreshments and comfort to us evacuees. That kindness left a deep impression on me during those difficult times.

The Great Depression hit hard around 1929 to 1932, and wages for farm laborers like me dropped from about 45-50 cents an hour to as low as 15 cents. Despite the hardships, I was fortunate to be independent and own a farm, which helped me manage through the tough economic times. Many Japanese struggled, but we supported each other.

During the Depression, many Japanese farmers who owned land had trouble making payments and lost their land to Bank of America. I leased land instead of owning it to avoid such risks. The bank was a significant factor in land ownership struggles for Japanese immigrants at that time.
The transcript does not mention Turlock, California, so I have no personal experience or stories related to that place.
